news_summary: Breakthroughs in LED technology, AI-driven energy optimization in electric motors, and innovative quantum imaging using sunlight showcase remarkable advancements reshaping energy efficiency and imaging capabilities.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans le domaine de la technologie LED, l'optimisation énergétique des moteurs électriques grâce à l'intelligence artificielle et l'imagerie quantique innovante utilisant la lumière du soleil témoignent de progrès remarquables qui redéfinissent l'efficacité énergétique et les capacités d'imagerie.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la tecnología LED, la optimización energética basada en la inteligencia artificial en los motores eléctricos y las innovadoras técnicas de imagen cuántica que utilizan la luz solar ponen de manifiesto notables progresos que están transformando la eficiencia energética y las capacidades de imagen.

articles.title: The “impossible” LED that could change everything
articles.summary: Scientists at the University of Cambridge have achieved what was once considered impossible by electrically powering insulating nanoparticles to create a completely new kind of LED. Using tiny organic “molecular antennas,” the team found a way to funnel energy into materials that normally cannot conduct electricity, producing ultra pure near infrared light with remarkable efficiency.
articles.content:
Scientists have developed a surprising new way to power materials that normally cannot conduct electricity, opening the door to a new generation of ultra pure near infrared LEDs for medical imaging, communications technology, and advanced sensors.

The breakthrough relies on tiny "molecular antennas" that funnel electrical energy into insulating nanoparticles. By using this method, researchers at the Cavendish Laboratory at the University of Cambridge created the first LEDs ever built from these previously "unpowerable" materials.

Their findings were published in Nature.

Molecular Antennas Power Insulating Nanoparticles

The research centers on lanthanide doped nanoparticles (LnNPs), materials known for producing exceptionally stable and highly pure light. They are especially valuable because they emit light in the second near infrared region, which can travel deep into biological tissue. This makes them attractive for medical imaging and sensing technologies.

Despite their optical advantages, these nanoparticles have one major drawback. They are electrical insulators, meaning they cannot easily carry electric current. That limitation has prevented scientists from using them in electronic devices such as LEDs.

Researchers at Cambridge found a way around that obstacle, a feat previously thought impossible under normal conditions. By attaching specially selected organic molecules to the nanoparticles, the team created a system capable of transferring electrical energy into the insulating material.

"These nanoparticles are fantastic light emitters, but we couldn't power them with electricity. It was a major barrier preventing their use in everyday technology," said Professor Akshay Rao, who led the research at the Cavendish Laboratory. "We've essentially found a back door to power them. The organic molecules act like antennas, catching charge carriers and then 'whispering' it to the nanoparticle through a special triplet energy transfer process, which is surprisingly efficient."

Organic Hybrid LEDs Achieve Over 98% Energy Transfer

To make the technology work, the scientists built a hybrid material that combines organic molecules with inorganic nanoparticles. They attached an organic dye called 9-anthracenecarboxylic acid (9-ACA) to the surface of the LnNPs.

Inside the newly designed LEDs, electrical charges are directed into the 9-ACA molecules instead of the nanoparticles themselves. These molecules act as molecular antennas that absorb the incoming energy and enter an excited "triplet state."

In many optical systems, triplet states are considered "dark" because their energy is often lost. In this new design, however, the triplet energy is transferred to the lanthanide ions inside the nanoparticles with more than 98% efficiency. That process causes the insulating nanoparticles to emit bright, highly pure light.

Ultra Pure Near Infrared LEDs With Low Power Use

The resulting devices, called "LnLEDs," operate at a relatively low voltage of about 5 volts. They also produce electroluminescence with an extremely narrow spectral width, giving them much purer light output than competing technologies such as quantum dots (QDs).

"The purity of the light in the second near-infrared window emitted by our LnLEDs is a huge advantage," said Dr. Zhongzheng Yu, a lead author of the study and postdoctoral research associate at the Cavendish Laboratory. "For applications like biomedical sensing or optical communications, you want a very sharp, specific wavelength. Our devices achieve this effortlessly, something that is very difficult to do with other materials."

Medical Imaging and Optical Communication Potential

The technology could lead to a wide range of future applications. Because the LEDs emit extremely pure near infrared light, they may enable new medical devices capable of seeing deep inside the body.

Tiny injectable or wearable LnLEDs could potentially help doctors detect cancers, monitor organs in real time, or activate light sensitive drugs with exceptional precision.

The narrow and stable light emission could also improve optical communications systems by reducing interference and allowing larger amounts of data to travel more clearly and efficiently. In addition, the technology may support highly sensitive detectors capable of identifying specific chemicals or biological markers.

First Generation Devices Already Show Strong Results

The research team has already achieved a peak external quantum efficiency greater than 0.6% for their NIR-II LEDs, an impressive result for an early generation device. The scientists also say there are clear paths for improving performance even further.

"This is just the beginning. We've unlocked a whole new class of materials for optoelectronics," added Dr. Yunzhou Deng, postdoctoral research associate at the Cavendish Laboratory. "The fundamental principle is so versatile that we can now explore countless combinations of organic molecules and insulating nanomaterials. This will allow us to create devices with tailored properties for applications we haven't even thought of yet."

The work received support in part from a UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) Frontier Research Grant (EP/Y015584/1) and Postdoctoral Individual Fellowships (Marie Skłodowska-Curie Fellowship grant scheme).
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers developed a novel method to electrically power insulating nanoparticles to create ultra pure near infrared LEDs. This innovation overcomes a major limitation in optoelectronics and has broad potential applications in medical imaging, optical communications, and advanced sensors, offering tangible benefits to society and healthcare. The story is detailed, focused on a single advancement, and highlights future impactful uses.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create new LED using previously unpowered nanoparticles
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long struggled to power special nanoparticles that produce very pure light but do not conduct electricity. These particles are useful for medical imaging and sensors but could not be used in LEDs (light-emitting diodes) because electricity could not flow through them.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Cambridge found a way to power these insulating nanoparticles by attaching tiny organic molecules called "molecular antennas." These molecules capture electrical energy and transfer it efficiently to the nanoparticles, making them light up. This method created the first LEDs from these materials, producing very pure near infrared light.

**Impact** – This breakthrough is important because it uses materials that were once thought impossible to power with electricity. The new LEDs work at low voltage and produce light with high purity, which is better than many current technologies. This could improve medical devices that see inside the body and help develop better ways to send data using light.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to explore many combinations of organic molecules and nanoparticles to improve the LEDs further. They aim to create devices that can be used in new medical tools, communication devices, and sensors that detect chemicals or biological markers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed a new way to power insulating nanoparticles, creating ultra pure near infrared LEDs that could advance medical imaging and communication technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une nouvelle LED à partir de nanoparticules auparavant non alimentées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont longtemps eu du mal à alimenter des nanoparticules spéciales qui produisent une lumière très pure mais qui ne conduisent pas l'électricité. Ces particules sont utiles pour l'imagerie médicale et les capteurs, mais ne pouvaient pas être utilisées dans les LED car l'électricité ne pouvait pas circuler à travers elles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Cambridge ont trouvé un moyen d’alimenter ces nanoparticules isolantes en attachant de petites molécules organiques appelées "antennes moléculaires." Ces molécules captent l’énergie électrique et la transfèrent efficacement aux nanoparticules, ce qui les fait briller. Cette méthode a permis de créer les premières LED à partir de ces matériaux, produisant une lumière proche infrarouge très pure.

**Impact** – Cette avancée est importante car elle utilise des matériaux auparavant considérés comme impossibles à alimenter électriquement. Les nouvelles LED fonctionnent à basse tension et produisent une lumière très pure, meilleure que de nombreuses technologies actuelles. Cela pourrait améliorer les dispositifs médicaux qui voient à l’intérieur du corps et aider à développer de meilleures façons de transmettre des données par la lumière.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit d’explorer de nombreuses combinaisons de molécules organiques et de nanoparticules pour améliorer encore les LED. Ils veulent créer des appareils pour de nouveaux outils médicaux, des dispositifs de communication et des capteurs capables de détecter des produits chimiques ou des marqueurs biologiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé une nouvelle façon d’alimenter des nanoparticules isolantes, créant des LED proche infrarouges ultra pures qui pourraient faire progresser l’imagerie médicale et les technologies de communication.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean un nuevo LED usando nanopartículas antes no alimentadas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han tenido dificultades para alimentar nanopartículas especiales que producen luz muy pura pero que no conducen electricidad. Estas partículas son útiles para imágenes médicas y sensores, pero no se podían usar en LEDs porque la electricidad no podía pasar a través de ellas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cambridge encontraron una forma de alimentar estas nanopartículas aislantes al unir pequeñas moléculas orgánicas llamadas "antenas moleculares." Estas moléculas capturan energía eléctrica y la transfieren eficientemente a las nanopartículas, haciéndolas brillar. Este método creó los primeros LEDs con estos materiales, produciendo luz cercana al infrarrojo muy pura.

**Impacto** – Este avance es importante porque usa materiales que antes se creían imposibles de alimentar con electricidad. Los nuevos LEDs funcionan con bajo voltaje y producen luz con alta pureza, mejor que muchas tecnologías actuales. Esto podría mejorar dispositivos médicos que ven dentro del cuerpo y ayudar a desarrollar mejores formas de enviar datos con luz.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea explorar muchas combinaciones de moléculas orgánicas y nanopartículas para mejorar aún más los LEDs. Quieren crear dispositivos para nuevas herramientas médicas, comunicaciones y sensores que detecten químicos o marcadores biológicos.

**Frase resumen** – Científicos han creado una nueva forma de alimentar nanopartículas aislantes, logrando LEDs de luz cercana al infrarrojo ultra pura que podrían avanzar en imágenes médicas y tecnologías de comunicación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of glowing, interconnected molecular antennae shaped like delicate organic tendrils gently funneling vibrant light into softly illuminated, crystalline nanoparticles that emit pure near-infrared light, set against a simple, natural background of subtle earth and soft blues to evoke innovation and deep biological insight.

articles.title: AI reveals the invisible magnetic chaos wasting energy inside electric motors
articles.summary: Electric vehicles are pushing scientists to tackle one of the biggest hidden energy drains inside electric motors: magnetic energy loss. Now, researchers in Japan have developed a powerful AI-driven physics model that can peer into the chaotic “maze-like” magnetic patterns inside motor materials and reveal how heat and microscopic magnetic structures trigger wasted energy.
articles.content:
The explosive growth of electric vehicles has intensified the search for ways to make electric motors more energy efficient. One major challenge is iron loss, also called magnetic hysteresis loss, which occurs when magnetic fields inside the motor repeatedly reverse direction. This process wastes energy as heat within the motor core, which is made from soft magnetic materials. Because electric motors often operate at high temperatures, thermal effects can also partially demagnetize these materials, making the energy loss problem even more complicated.

A key factor behind these effects is the behavior of magnetic domains, which are tiny magnetic regions inside materials. The arrangement and structure of these domains strongly affect how magnetic materials respond to heat and how much energy they lose during operation.

Complex Magnetic Maze Domains

Some soft magnetic materials contain highly intricate magnetic structures called maze domains, named for their zig-zag, labyrinth-like appearance. These maze domains can change abruptly as temperatures rise or fall, influencing how energy is lost in the material. However, scientists have struggled to fully understand these structures because many interacting factors are involved, including the material's microscopic structure, thermal effects, and energy stability.

To better understand this behavior, researchers led by Professor Masato Kotsugi and Dr. Ken Masuzawa from the Department of Material Science and Technology at Tokyo University of Science (TUS), Japan, worked with collaborators from the University of Tsukuba, Okayama University, and Kyoto University to develop a new model called the entropy-feature-eXtended Ginzburg-Landau (eX-GL) model. The team used this approach to study the energy landscape of maze domains in a rare-earth iron garnet (RIG).

"Conventional simulations oversimplify real materials, while experiments reveal complexity without a clear way to quantify cause and effect," explains Prof. Kotsugi. "Our physics-based explainable artificial intelligence framework addresses these limitations and is designed to mechanistically explain temperature-dependent magnetization reversal process."

Their findings were published in the journal Scientific Reports.

AI and Physics Reveal Hidden Magnetic Behavior

To explore how temperature affects magnetization removal in maze domains, the researchers captured microscopic images of the magnetic domains in the RIG sample at different temperatures. These images were then analyzed using the eX-GL model.

The first stage of the model uses persistent homology (PH), a sophisticated mathematical method that identifies topological features within data. This allowed the team to detect uneven structural characteristics in the magnetic domain images. Next, machine learning-based pattern recognition was used to determine the most important features from the PH data, producing a digital free-energy landscape that tracks how magnetic microstructures evolve as energy changes. Finally, mathematical analysis linked these microscopic domain structures to the larger magnetization reversal process.

Using this method, the researchers identified a dominant feature known as PC1, which successfully captured the magnetization reversal process. By connecting PC1 with physical properties, the team visualized four major energy barriers that strongly influence magnetization reversal dynamics.

Hidden Energy Barriers Inside Magnetic Materials

A detailed analysis of these barriers and the related microstructures revealed how different forms of energy affect magnetization reversal. The researchers measured energy transfer involving exchange interactions, demagnetizing effects, and entropy.

They also discovered that maze domains grow more complex as the length of domain walls increases. This increasing complexity is driven by interactions between entropy and exchange forces. These results helped clarify the physical mechanisms behind maze-domain reversal behavior.

"Our eX-GL approach effectively automates the interpretation of complex magnetization reversal process and enables identification of hidden mechanisms, difficult to discern using conventional methods," says Prof. Kotsugi. "In addition, since free energy is a universal thermodynamic metric, our model can be extended to other systems with similar characteristics."

Overall, the study not only sheds light on the mechanics of maze domains, but also introduces a broader strategy for investigating complex energy landscapes in magnetic systems and other related physical materials.

This research was supported by a Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (KAKENHI) Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (A) (21H04656). Additional support came from JST-CREST (Grant No. JPMJCR21O1). C. Mitsumata received support from the Tsukuba Research Center for Energy Materials Science (TREMS) at the University of Tsukuba.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough using AI to understand and reduce magnetic energy loss in electric motors, which has broad implications for improving energy efficiency in electric vehicles. This advancement addresses a major hidden energy drain, contributing to more sustainable transportation technology and energy conservation. The research is detailed, focused, and offers a novel, explainable AI-driven physics model with potential wide applicability.
articles.personality_title: AI uncovers hidden magnetic energy loss inside electric motors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Electric vehicles are becoming more popular, but their electric motors lose energy because of invisible magnetic changes inside the motor materials. This loss wastes power as heat, making motors less efficient.

**What happened** – Scientists in Japan developed a new artificial intelligence (AI) model that looks closely at tiny magnetic patterns called maze domains inside motor materials. Using this AI-driven physics model, they studied how temperature and small magnetic structures cause energy to be wasted. Their research combined advanced math, machine learning, and real images of magnetic materials.

**Impact** – This work reveals the complex ways magnetic parts inside motors change and lose energy, something hard to see before. Understanding these hidden energy losses helps engineers find ways to build motors that waste less energy. This is important because it can improve electric vehicle efficiency and reduce power waste.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to apply their AI model to other magnetic materials and systems. This could lead to better motor designs and new technologies that save energy in many devices, not just electric vehicles.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New AI-powered research reveals hidden magnetic energy losses inside motors, paving the way for more efficient electric vehicles.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA révèle les pertes magnétiques cachées dans les moteurs électriques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les véhicules électriques se multiplient, mais leurs moteurs perdent de l’énergie à cause de changements magnétiques invisibles dans les matériaux du moteur. Cette perte transforme l’énergie en chaleur, ce qui réduit l’efficacité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques japonais ont créé un nouveau modèle d’intelligence artificielle (IA) qui analyse de près de petits motifs magnétiques appelés domaines labyrinthe dans les matériaux des moteurs. Grâce à ce modèle basé sur la physique et l’IA, ils ont étudié comment la température et ces structures magnétiques microscopiques provoquent des pertes d’énergie. Leur recherche combine mathématiques avancées, apprentissage automatique et images réelles.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre comment les parties magnétiques complexes à l’intérieur des moteurs changent et perdent de l’énergie, ce qu’il était difficile de comprendre auparavant. Comprendre ces pertes cachées aide les ingénieurs à fabriquer des moteurs qui gaspillent moins d’énergie. C’est important pour améliorer l’efficacité des véhicules électriques et réduire le gaspillage.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche veut appliquer ce modèle d’IA à d’autres matériaux magnétiques et systèmes. Cela pourrait conduire à de meilleurs moteurs et à de nouvelles technologies économes en énergie pour plusieurs appareils, pas seulement les véhicules électriques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche utilisant l’IA révèle les pertes magnétiques cachées dans les moteurs, ouvrant la voie à des véhicules électriques plus efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA revela pérdidas magnéticas ocultas en motores eléctricos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los vehículos eléctricos están creciendo, pero sus motores pierden energía debido a cambios magnéticos invisibles dentro de los materiales del motor. Esta pérdida convierte energía en calor, lo que reduce la eficiencia.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos en Japón desarrollaron un nuevo modelo de inteligencia artificial (IA) que examina de cerca pequeños patrones magnéticos llamados dominios laberínticos dentro de los materiales del motor. Usando este modelo basado en física e IA, estudiaron cómo la temperatura y estas estructuras magnéticas microscópicas causan pérdida de energía. Su investigación combina matemáticas avanzadas, aprendizaje automático e imágenes reales.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo muestra cómo las partes magnéticas complejas dentro de los motores cambian y pierden energía, algo difícil de entender antes. Comprender estas pérdidas ocultas ayuda a los ingenieros a fabricar motores que desperdician menos energía. Esto es importante para mejorar la eficiencia de los vehículos eléctricos y reducir el desperdicio.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea aplicar su modelo de IA a otros materiales y sistemas magnéticos. Esto podría llevar a mejores diseños de motores y nuevas tecnologías que ahorren energía en muchos dispositivos, no solo en vehículos eléctricos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva investigación con IA revela pérdidas magnéticas ocultas en motores, abriendo camino a vehículos eléctricos más eficientes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned abstract painting of a labyrinthine maze formed by glowing, interwoven magnetic domain walls inside a softly glowing electric motor core, with subtle thermal waves gently flowing through the maze to symbolize heat and energy transfer, all rendered in natural, muted colors with intricate patterns representing hidden energy barriers and dynamic magnetic interactions.

articles.title: Quantum ghost imaging works using only sunlight in stunning new experiment
articles.summary: Scientists have achieved something that once sounded almost impossible: using ordinary sunlight to create quantum-linked photon pairs, a phenomenon normally dependent on precise laboratory lasers. By building a sun-tracking system that funnels sunlight through optical fiber into a special crystal, researchers generated strongly correlated photons capable of performing “ghost imaging,” where images are reconstructed indirectly through quantum correlations. Remarkably, the sunlight-powered setup produced image quality close to that of a traditional laser system, even recreating detailed images like a “ghost face.”
articles.content:
Correlated and entangled photon pairs are essential tools in quantum optics. Scientists usually create these photon pairs through a process called spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC), in which a powerful, highly stable laser shines into a nonlinear crystal. Because SPDC depends so heavily on coherent laser light, researchers have long considered the technique impractical outside carefully controlled laboratory environments.

More recently, studies have shown that perfectly coherent light is not actually required for SPDC to work. Even partially coherent light sources can produce correlated photon pairs, while also transferring some of their own coherence properties to the generated photons. That discovery led researchers to ask an intriguing question: could sunlight itself be used to generate correlated photon pairs?

Using Sunlight for Quantum Optics

Turning sunlight into a usable SPDC source comes with major obstacles. Sunlight reaching Earth constantly fluctuates in brightness, direction, and position, making it difficult to maintain the precise alignment needed for SPDC experiments and photon detection.

At the same time, sunlight offers a major advantage. Unlike lasers, it does not require electrical power or complex laboratory equipment. A sunlight-based system could potentially operate in remote locations or even in space where traditional laser systems may be impractical.

A research team led by Wuhong Zhang and Lixiang Chen at Xiamen University has now demonstrated a working solution. Writing in Advanced Photonics, the scientists described an experimental setup that uses sunlight as the only pump source for SPDC.

Their system includes an automatic sun-tracking device similar to an equatorial telescope mount. The tracker continuously follows the Sun throughout the day and directs sunlight into a 20 m plastic multimode optical fiber. The fiber transports the light into a dark indoor laboratory, where it pumps a periodically poled potassium titanyl phosphate (PPKTP) nonlinear crystal.

Sunlight Successfully Produces Correlated Photon Pairs

Despite the instability of natural sunlight, the setup successfully generated photon pairs with strong position correlations. To test the system, the researchers used the photon pairs for ghost imaging, a quantum imaging technique in which images are reconstructed using correlated photons instead of direct spatial detection.

The sunlight-driven system achieved a ghost-imaging visibility of 90.7%, close to the 95.5 percent visibility produced by a standard 405 nm laser operating at the same pump power.

Beyond simple double-slit imaging, the researchers also reconstructed a more detailed two-dimensional image described as a "ghost face." The result demonstrated that the sunlight-powered system could handle more complex spatial patterns.

According to the researchers, sunlight's broad spectrum helps support quasi-phase matching inside the nonlinear crystal, allowing the production of large numbers of position-correlated photon pairs. By collecting data over extended periods, the team improved both the signal-to-noise and contrast-to-noise ratios, showing that the system could maintain stable performance despite natural fluctuations in sunlight.

A Fully Passive Quantum Imaging System

The experiment marks the first successful demonstration of sunlight-pumped SPDC combined with ghost imaging. By removing the need for lasers and external electrical power, the system creates a fully passive source of correlated photon pairs.

The researchers believe the technology could prove especially useful for future quantum imaging and quantum information systems used in remote environments or space-based applications.

They also noted that advances in sunlight collection, crystal engineering, and image reconstruction methods, including compressed sensing and machine learning, could further improve image quality and imaging speed while helping move the technology closer to practical real-world use.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers successfully used ordinary sunlight to generate quantum-linked photon pairs for ghost imaging, a process previously reliant on precise lasers. This innovation removes the need for complex, power-consuming equipment, enabling quantum imaging in remote or space environments. The experiment demonstrates broad potential impact for quantum technologies beyond controlled labs, with detailed context and promising future applications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use sunlight to create quantum images without lasers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum imaging usually needs special lasers to create pairs of linked photons, tiny particles of light. These pairs help make images in a special way called ghost imaging. Until now, this process could only work inside labs with precise equipment.

**What happened** – A team of scientists at Xiamen University built a system that uses sunlight instead of lasers to create these linked photon pairs. They designed a sun-tracking device that directs sunlight into a crystal through a long optical fiber. This setup successfully produced photon pairs that could create clear ghost images, including detailed pictures like a "ghost face." The quality was almost as good as with traditional laser systems.

**Impact** – Using sunlight means the system does not need electricity or complex machines, making it much simpler and cheaper. This breakthrough shows that quantum imaging can work outside labs, possibly in remote places or even in space. It also proves that sunlight's natural light can create the special linked photons needed for advanced quantum experiments.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to improve how much sunlight the system can collect and refine the crystals and image processing methods. They are exploring ways to make the images clearer and the process faster, using tools like machine learning. These improvements could help make sunlight-based quantum imaging practical for real-world uses.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have shown that ordinary sunlight can replace lasers to create quantum-linked photons, opening new possibilities for quantum imaging without complex equipment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques utilisent la lumière du soleil pour créer des images quantiques sans lasers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'imagerie quantique nécessite généralement des lasers spéciaux pour créer des paires de photons liés, de minuscules particules de lumière. Ces paires aident à former des images d'une manière particulière appelée imagerie fantôme. Jusqu'à présent, ce procédé ne fonctionnait que dans des laboratoires équipés de matériel précis.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs de l'université de Xiamen a construit un système qui utilise la lumière du soleil au lieu des lasers pour créer ces paires de photons liés. Ils ont conçu un dispositif de suivi du soleil qui dirige la lumière solaire dans un cristal via une longue fibre optique. Ce montage a réussi à produire des paires de photons capables de créer des images fantômes nettes, y compris des images détaillées comme un « visage fantôme ». La qualité était presque aussi bonne qu'avec des systèmes laser traditionnels.

**Impact** – Utiliser la lumière du soleil signifie que le système n'a pas besoin d'électricité ni de machines complexes, ce qui le rend beaucoup plus simple et moins coûteux. Cette avancée montre que l'imagerie quantique peut fonctionner hors des laboratoires, peut-être dans des endroits reculés ou même dans l'espace. Elle prouve aussi que la lumière naturelle du soleil peut créer les photons liés nécessaires pour des expériences quantiques avancées.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer la collecte de la lumière solaire, affiner les cristaux et les méthodes de traitement des images. Ils explorent des moyens de rendre les images plus claires et le processus plus rapide, en utilisant des outils comme l'apprentissage automatique. Ces améliorations pourraient rendre l'imagerie quantique solaire pratique pour des applications réelles.

**En une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont démontré que la lumière du soleil ordinaire peut remplacer les lasers pour créer des photons quantiques liés, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour l'imagerie quantique sans équipement complexe.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan la luz solar para crear imágenes cuánticas sin láseres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La imagen cuántica normalmente necesita láseres especiales para crear pares de fotones enlazados, pequeñas partículas de luz. Estos pares ayudan a formar imágenes de una manera especial llamada imagen fantasma. Hasta ahora, este proceso solo funcionaba en laboratorios con equipos precisos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de científicos de la Universidad de Xiamen construyó un sistema que usa la luz solar en lugar de láseres para crear estos pares de fotones enlazados. Diseñaron un dispositivo que sigue al sol y dirige la luz solar a través de una fibra óptica larga hacia un cristal. Este sistema logró producir pares de fotones que pudieron crear imágenes fantasma claras, incluyendo imágenes detalladas como una "cara fantasma". La calidad fue casi tan buena como con los sistemas láser tradicionales.

**Impacto** – Usar la luz del sol significa que el sistema no necesita electricidad ni máquinas complejas, haciéndolo mucho más simple y barato. Este avance muestra que la imagen cuántica puede funcionar fuera de los laboratorios, posiblemente en lugares remotos o incluso en el espacio. También demuestra que la luz natural del sol puede crear los fotones enlazados necesarios para experimentos cuánticos avanzados.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren mejorar la cantidad de luz solar que el sistema puede captar y perfeccionar los cristales y los métodos de procesamiento de imágenes. Están explorando formas de hacer las imágenes más claras y el proceso más rápido, usando herramientas como el aprendizaje automático. Estas mejoras podrían ayudar a que la imagen cuántica basada en luz solar sea práctica para usos reales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos demostraron que la luz solar común puede reemplazar a los láseres para crear fotones cuánticos enlazados, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para la imagen cuántica sin equipos complejos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing sunbeam gently captured by a delicate, mechanical sun-tracking device that channels the light through a translucent fiber optic cable into a softly illuminated dark room, where a crystalline structure emits pairs of shimmering, interconnected light orbs symbolizing entangled photons, all rendered in natural, soft earth tones and subtle golden hues.
